steps.title: Use equal radii
steps.content: Points $M$ and $N$ lie on a circle centered at $C$, so $CM$ and $CN$ are radii of the same circle. That makes triangle $CMN$ isosceles.

steps.title: Identify radii create an isosceles triangle
steps.content: Since M and N lie on the circle with center C, the distances $CM$ and $CN$ are both radii of the same circle. Therefore $CM = CN$, which means triangle $CMN$ is isosceles.

steps.title: Apply the base angle property
steps.content: In an isosceles triangle, the angles opposite the equal sides are equal. The two equal sides are $CM$ and $CN$, so the angles opposite them—namely $\angle CMN$ and $\angle CNM$ (equivalently, $\angle MNC$)—must be equal. Let's call this angle $x$.

steps.title: Use angle sum to solve for the base angle
steps.content:
The three angles of triangle $CMN$ sum to $180^{\circ}$:
$$\angle MCN + \angle CMN + \angle MNC = 180^{\circ}$$
Substituting $\angle MCN = 84^{\circ}$ and $\angle CMN = \angle MNC = x$:
$$84^{\circ} + x + x = 180^{\circ}$$
$$84^{\circ} + 2x = 180^{\circ}$$
$$2x = 96^{\circ}$$
$$x = 48^{\circ}$$
Therefore $\angle MNC = 48^{\circ}$, and the answer is 48.
